2.blurb: Hongshan, in what is now northeastern China around Liaoning, is the earliest place on earth where people built hilltop stone altars, a goddess-temple, and ritual pits stuffed with exquisitely carved jade pig-dragons and coiled cloud-dragons — all around 3500 BCE, long before any Chinese dynasty existed. It's the first Chinese culture to act like it has full-blown organized religion.

While Eridu was stacking its first mud temple around 5000 BCE, the farmers of Mehrgarh in what is now Pakistan had already been growing wheat and barley, herding cattle, making cotton thread, and smelting copper for almost two thousand years. The Indus story starts earlier than Mesopotamia's first real cities — the Harappans were running their own deep Neolithic civilization in parallel, just with no writing to leave us a receipt.

3.blurb: Roughly twelve centuries after Uruk, the Indus Valley produced something just as astonishing: Mohenjo-daro and Harappa, planned brick cities laid out on a grid, with standardized bricks, covered drains, public bathhouses, and private toilets in individual houses. No palaces, no royal tombs, no obvious kings — a very different kind of urban revolution from the temple-run city-states of Sumer.

Erlitou, on the Yi River in central China around 1900–1550 BCE, is the oldest site in China big enough and organized enough to call a proper city — palaces on rammed-earth platforms, bronze workshops casting ritual vessels, and a planned center whose layout maps almost perfectly onto the legendary Xia dynasty the Chinese texts insisted was real. It's where Chinese legend finally meets archaeology.

4.matchText: Harappan burials from the same period
4.blurb: At Harappa and Mohenjo-daro, the graves look almost embarrassingly modest next to Ur's gold-drenched death pits: a pot or two, some shell bangles, a copper mirror, and the occasional bead necklace, spread evenly across rich and poor. No ruler was buried with stacks of treasure, no attendants were killed to follow anyone into the afterlife — whoever ran the Indus cities, they seem to have done it without making a show of it in death.

Shimao, a massive stone-walled town on the loess plateau around 2000 BCE, is one of the biggest and strangest late-Neolithic sites in China — and the builders buried dozens of sacrificed young women in pits beneath the city walls as foundation offerings, their decapitated heads arranged in neat rows. It's the same grim logic as Ur's death pits, on the opposite end of Eurasia, at roughly the same moment.

The 74 sacrificed attendants in Queen Puabi's death pit at Ur are the best-known Bronze Age example of royal retainer sacrifice — and they are an order of magnitude smaller than what was happening at the same time far upriver on the Nile. At Kerma in Upper Nubia, the largest royal tumuli contained three to four hundred sacrificed retainers arranged in corridors radiating out from the central burial chamber. The Indus Valley, alone among the four Bronze Age cradles, did not practice retainer sacrifice at all.

5.blurb: The great Indus cities didn't burn and didn't get conquered — they just quietly emptied out between about 1900 and 1300 BCE. The Sarasvati river system dried up, the monsoon weakened, trade with Mesopotamia unraveled, and the Harappans scattered into smaller villages eastward. No single catastrophe, just a civilization slowly running out of the water and the connections that had made it possible.

Liangzhu, in the wet rice country south of the Yangtze, was pulling off something the Yellow River wouldn't manage for another thousand years: a full-scale state, with a jade-wearing elite, enormous earthen dams holding back seasonal floods, and a carefully planned ceremonial center. Then, around 2300 BCE, the whole system drowned — the dams failed, the center was abandoned, and China's first experiment with complex statehood ended underwater.

The gold Gudea was importing came out of the Wadi Allaqi valley east of the Nile in Lower Nubia — the same gold-bearing rock formations that would later bankroll the New Kingdom Egyptian treasury and fill the Kassite kings' requests in the Amarna Letters. The Nubian gold pipeline ran from Sumerian Lagash all the way through to Kassite Babylon, a millennium and a half of continuous extraction.

6.blurb: The Indus cities collapsed on almost the exact same timetable as Ur III. Mohenjo-daro's population thinned out, Harappa's bathhouse and granaries were abandoned, the standardized weights and seals stopped being made, and the long-distance trade with Meluhha that had filled Ur's royal tombs with carnelian beads simply dried up. Two of the three great Bronze Age cradles went quiet within a single generation.

8.blurb: After the Harappan cities emptied out, a new culture arrives on the Indian subcontinent around 1500 BCE: horse-driving, chariot-riding Indo-European speakers composing the hymns that became the Rigveda, praising gods named Indra, Mitra, and Varuna — the same gods Mitanni's ruling class was invoking in treaties with Egypt at the same moment. The Vedic world and the Near Eastern great-power club were two ends of the same cultural thread.

The gold the Kassite kings and their fellow Great Kings were begging the pharaoh for in the Amarna Letters was specifically Nubian gold. Egypt's colonial province in Nubia, run by the Viceroy of Kush out of the administrative town of Aniba, was producing gold on an industrial scale from the Wadi Allaqi mines, and the pharaoh's ability to say yes or no to his fellow kings depended entirely on the flow out of those mines. The Amarna Letters are the diplomatic other end of Nubia's gold pipeline.

13.blurb: While Mesopotamia was inventing writing, law, and empire between 5000 and 1550 BCE, China was running its own completely independent experiment: millet and rice domesticated in two separate centers, Yangshao painted pottery, Hongshan jade, the drowned Liangzhu state, Longshan walled towns, the Yu the Great flood legend, and finally Erlitou — the first Chinese city big enough to match the legends of the Xia dynasty. Different script, different cities, different gods, and no Mesopotamian input anywhere along the way.

7.blurb: Donkey caravans were the Bronze Age's standard solution for bulk overland trade. Five hundred years before the Assyrian merchants were hauling tin and textiles up to Kanesh, the Egyptian official Harkhuf was leading caravans of three hundred donkeys south into Nubia for ebony, ivory, incense, and exotic goods — one of the earliest and best-documented donkey-caravan operations in human history.

11.blurb: Taharqa was a Kushite pharaoh of Egypt's 25th Dynasty — a descendant of the Egyptianized Nubian elite who inherited Egypt's former Nubian province after the Bronze Age collapse and, three centuries later, marched north and conquered Egypt itself. Ancient Nubia Ch 8 tells the setup: how the bicultural Nubian nobility absorbed the cult of Amun at Jebel Barkal and carried it forward when Egypt's state apparatus withdrew around 1070 BCE.
